title: Rationale for Medication Assisted Treatment
author: Ned

seo.title: Rationale for Medication Assisted Treatment | Plan Your Recovery

body:
Medications help us target the underlying neuropathways that cause addictive behavior.

For decades, there has been ambivalence in the treatment and recovery communities about how to treat addiction. 12 step and other mutual support groups often have a strong distrust for medications. They are especially skeptical of those that cause physical dependence. Many individuals in these programs wonder, “why would I use drugs to treat a problem that was caused by drugs? Shouldn’t I try to handle this on my own?”

There are three FDA approved medications for alcohol use disorder, none of which are addictive. They help reduce cravings for alcohol, as a person habituates to living without intoxication. Unfortunately, skepticism towards these treatments and misplaced optimism about self reliance is abundant in the treatment world. This has led to chronic underutilization of medications in the treatment of substance and alcohol use disorders. Many people believe that they can muscle through an addictive disorder, correcting what they perceive to be a moral failing. Still, evidence tells us that this simply isn’t true for hundreds of thousands of individuals.

In order to explain why, we must first understand the neuroscience of addiction.

Two systems are important to understanding addiciton -- the executive system and the limbic system.

There are two primary brain systems involved in motivational regulation: the executive system and the limbic system. The executive system is the seat of all conscious processing.  The executive system plans, reasons, sets goals, and weighs costs and benefits. It uses more stable features of consciousness—e.g. values, principles, and identity—as a way to make key judgments and decisions. Using our executive system, we engage in consequential thinking. We play out hypothetical scenarios to determine which path of action will best achieve our desired results.

The limbic system is evolutionarily older than the prefrontal cortex. It includes areas that regulate our:

  • Appetites
  • Attachments
  • Emotional responses
  • Memories
  • Reward salience.

Sometimes called the “survival” or “pleasure” center of the brain, the limbic system helps the organism to identify immediate rewards and dangers and respond to them successfully. These responses are more rapid and automatic than the cognitive processes of the executive system.

One of the primary roles of the executive system is to inhibit problematic limbic system impulses. These impulses conflict with our higher level values— our long-term goals, our relationships, and our sense of self. I override my desire to turn over and go to sleep in the morning because I give deliberate priority to being on time. I don’t eat every delicious looking cookie I see because I value my health. The regulatory inhibitions of the prefrontal cortex help us to stabilize our lives and secure the things we want over the long-term. To achieve this stability, we often have to forgo immediate rewards.

Cookies are delicious, but having too many is bad for our health. We utilize our executive systems to protect our bodies.

Addictive substances and behaviors are capable of creating an imbalance between the limbic system and the prefrontal cortex. This imbalance means that higher level values are sacrificed to limbic system drives. Parents with heroin addiction will often say that they want to be good parents (conscious value processed by the executive system). The drive to use heroin (limbic system over-ride) subverts this value over and over again.

Few of us without addiction have ever experienced the type of limbic system over-ride that drives addictive behavior. But imagine that you are caught in the wilderness without food and water. You come across a pool but there is a sign on front of it that reads “POISON: DO NOT DRINK.” The water looks clean and fresh. As time passes, your mouth becomes scratchy and dry. You crave liquid more than you ever have craved anything else before in your life. Eventually, the drive to survive becomes too strong, and your limbic system pushes you to consume the water that may lead to your death.

If you were dying of thirst, would you be able to ignore a pool of water?

Addiction tricks the brain into believing that alcohol or drugs are necessary for survival. The drive to use becomes as powerful as the drive to find food and water. The executive system’s longer term goals such as maintaining healthy relationships, pursuing educational or professional objectives, or maintaining one’s reputation and legal status are all overridden by the drive to use.

Described in this way, addiction feels nearly insurmountable. It no longer feels surprising when we consider how many people relapse after attempting sobriety through willpower alone. Fortunately, medications can help level the playing field. By altering our neurochemistry, medication, along with the rest of a well-rounded treatment plan, can make the impossible possible.

To learn more about the neurochemical pathways of each medication for substance use disorders and alcohol use disorders, please follow the links below. If you have further questions about how medication can be used to support your recovery goals, email us at npresnall@planyourrecovery.com or call us at 314-222-5896.
